notes: Test prompts for the explain_the_repo skill. The skill produces architecture documentation that explains a codebase — combining prose sections with multi-diagram Mermaid sets via a doc-level design pass. Two eval levels: doc-level prompts (test the outer skill end-to-end) and diagram-only prompts (test the inner diagram procedure standalone). Diagram-only prompts are kept because the inner procedure is also useful on its own for 'draw a diagram of X' requests.

evals.name: explain-the-repo-architecture-doc
evals.prompt: Explain the autoresearch repo at ~/Github/autoresearch — give me an architecture doc I can hand to a new engineer.
expected_to_include: A doc plan from step 0 naming the sections (architecture overview, where-to-start, component summaries, etc.) before generation
A `Where to start reading` section with 3-6 file pointers, each with a one-sentence purpose
An architecture-overview section that invokes the inner diagram procedure (likely producing a multi-diagram set for this complex agent system)
Component summaries grounded in named files (not fabricated)
An `Out of scope` section at the end naming what's deliberately not covered and where to find it
A `<details>` Generation notes block at the very end naming the doc plan, the doc-panel verdict (or skipped), and the per-diagram panel results
expected_to_exclude: Install instructions (those belong in README, not architecture doc)
Function-by-function code documentation
A doc with no Where-to-start section (the section is non-optional)
Fabricated file paths or component names not grounded in the actual repo
More than 10 sections (cap rule from doc-design-pass)

evals.name: explain-the-repo-simple-api
evals.prompt: Architecture doc for an AWS Lambda + Serverless service that exposes a Data Gateway API in front of an in-process GraphQL engine. Clients call /v0/query?queryId=..., the Lambda loads a persisted query template from disk, executes it, and resolvers fan out to upstream HTTP services.
expected_to_include: Step 0 design pass concluding on the minimal-shape doc (3-4 sections)
Headline + where-to-start + architecture overview (single-diagram set)
Component summaries section (the system has 3-4 named components)
Out of scope section
Doc-panel critique skipped (3-or-fewer-substantive-section doc)
expected_to_exclude: Doc-panel critique surfacing in the output (it's skipped for this size)
A multi-diagram set in the architecture overview (the system warrants only one diagram)
A Lifecycle section (no offline / online split in this system)

evals.name: explain-the-repo-ml-system
evals.prompt: Document a Keras two-tower recommendation system: offline training writes model + preprocessor + item-embedding artifacts; online serving loads them and answers recommend(user_features, top_k) calls via a user tower forward pass plus FAISS ANN search over the precomputed item embeddings.
expected_to_include: Step 0 design pass concluding on a 5-section doc with a Lifecycle section (the offline/online split warrants its own section)
Headline + where-to-start + architecture overview + lifecycle + out-of-scope
Lifecycle section explicitly distinguishing offline pipeline from online runtime, with the artifact handoff as the boundary
Component summaries grounded in named files
Single-diagram architecture overview (both lifecycles can fit one frame with the handoff visible)
expected_to_exclude: An attempt to draw the full training pipeline as part of the architecture overview's diagram (that's lifecycle-section content)
Forcing a 'request' frame onto the offline pipeline (it's not request-shaped)
Mixing online serving and offline training in one diagram without clear boundary

evals.name: diagram-only-multi-tenant-rag
evals.prompt: Draw a system diagram for a request flowing through a multi-tenant retrieval-augmented generation service. The platform has an API gateway that does PII redaction, a shared orchestrator with plan/execute/verify/adjust stages, an LLM router with cost tiers, retriever adapters that hit a tenant-scoped vector store, an eval harness, a feedback pipeline that updates a skill registry, and a per-tenant hash-chained audit log.
expected_to_include: Direct invocation of the inner diagram procedure (no doc-level wrapper)
Step 0 (diagram-set design pass) concluding N=1
Three subgraphs encoding trust boundary
A single trace from the API client back to the API client
Audit log as a single sink with multiple dotted incoming arrows
PII redaction as an edge label
Plan-execute-verify-adjust loop drawn as a real loop
A panel summary at the end
expected_to_exclude: A full architecture doc (the user asked for a diagram only, not a doc)
A `Where to start reading` section (this is diagram-only)
Diagram-set design panel critique (skipped for N=1)

evals.name: diagram-only-multi-cadence-agent
evals.prompt: Draw a system diagram for an AI agent that runs a multi-phase pipeline: each request triggers a deep-dive phase that branches between an orchestrated path and a single-call path, then source analysis, story generation, and evaluation. The agent has an intra-run refinement loop and a cross-run feedback loop. State persists across runs in a finding registry, an exception registry, and a per-run outputs directory.
expected_to_include: Direct invocation of the inner diagram procedure
Step 0 producing N>1 (3-5 diagrams)
A headline trace as one diagram in the set
A zoom sibling for the orchestrated-vs-single-call dispatch
A cadence sibling for the cross-run feedback loop
Possibly a topology sibling for the persistent state
A diagram-set panel summary
Per-diagram panel summaries
expected_to_exclude: A single 20+ node diagram trying to cover everything
Hard failure paths drawn as a peer of the happy path
A full architecture doc (user asked for diagram only)

evals.name: iteration-mode-redraw
evals.expected_set_size: 1 or N depending on whether the original was sprawling because it should be a multi-diagram set
evals.prompt: Here's a diagram of our checkout flow [user describes a layered diagram with 12+ boxes, decorative color, audit logging drawn as a peer service, and missing return arrows]. It's hard to follow — can you redraw it?
expected_to_include: Diagnosis BEFORE redrawing: 3-4 named failure modes from the diagnostic checklist
Step 0 design pass deciding whether the redraw should be a single diagram or a multi-diagram set (often the original is sprawling because it should be a set)
Plan section per diagram
Redrawn diagram(s) following the procedure
A panel summary per diagram
expected_to_exclude: Going straight to redraw without diagnosis
Reproducing the original's failure modes in the redraw

evals.name: out-of-scope-erd
evals.prompt: Draw me an ER diagram of user, post, and comment tables in a typical blog schema.
expected_to_include: Recognition that this is an ERD, not a system architecture diagram or doc
Direct use of Mermaid erDiagram syntax (or recommendation thereof)
Either no invocation of the skill, or the skill recognizing the case and stepping aside
expected_to_exclude: Forcing tables into a flowchart
Going through any design-pass procedure for what is not a system-architecture concern
Producing a full architecture doc for an ERD request

evals.name: explicit-opt-out
evals.prompt: Quick architecture overview, no critique, just sketch it: webhook handler that validates signatures, enqueues to SQS, has a worker Lambda that writes to DynamoDB.
expected_to_include: Step 0 detecting the opt-out signal ('quick', 'no critique', 'just sketch') and dropping to the minimum 3-section shape
Headline + architecture overview (single diagram) + where-to-start (or out-of-scope as a placeholder)
Doc-panel skipped
Inner diagram procedure's panel critique skipped (per opt-out)
Inner diagram procedure's syntax linter STILL ran (rendering correctness is non-negotiable)
expected_to_exclude: Two parallel diagram-panel subagents
A doc-panel critique
A multi-section doc beyond the minimum (user opted for quick)
